notes.note: The formal epistolary heading: "Cicero to Appius, imperator, sends greeting." IMP. (imperator) was the honorific won by a victorious commander and saluted by his troops; Appius held it from his Cilician campaign. S. D. abbreviates salutem dicit.

A libertus was a manumitted slave who remained bound to his former master as patron; trusted freedmen routinely managed a Roman grandee's correspondence and confidential business, as Phania does for Appius.

notes.anchor: not unwillingly --- nor unwilling, I trust, will Minerva be
notes.note: A play on the proverb invita Minerva ("with Minerva unwilling," i.e. against one's natural aptitude); Minerva was the goddess of wisdom and craft.

notes.anchor: I shall name not only \textit{[Greek: Polias]} but \textit{[Greek: Appias]}
notes.note: Polias ("of the city") was Athena's chief Athenian cult-title; Cicero coins "Appias" as a flattering parallel, joking that a Minerva-statue taken from Appius's household would be named after Appius himself.
A iurisconsultus was an expert in civil law who advised litigants and drafted legal formulas; Cicero puns that this Valerius needs better legal protection than he gives others.
